Event: Patrick is permanently kicked out of his parents’ house at a young age
Explanation: Parents harm their child and gain no benefit described.

Event: Patrick reunites with his real parents and holds no hard feelings
Explanation: Reconciliation benefits his own well-being and their relationship.

Event: Patrick attacks patrons at the Krusty Krab thinking one is a supervillain
Explanation: He harms innocents and gets no benefit aside from transient delusion.

Event: Patrick runs faster than a brakeless train to keep up with it
Explanation: His feat of speed implies self-preservation and likely helps avert a disaster.

Event: Patrick starts taking things from citizens as “king,” amassing tribute
Explanation: He gains a large amount of stuff while unfairly depriving others.

Event: Patrick orders Squidward’s house demolished for a Ferris wheel
Explanation: He indulges his whim at Squidward’s major expense while enjoying power.

Event: Construction crew and citizens stop obeying Patrick and walk away
Explanation: They cut their losses, regain property, and remove his illegitimate power.

Event: Patrick eats his own hand along with food
Explanation: He mildly harms himself for no clear extra gain beyond normal eating.

Event: Patrick goes on a rampage at the Valentine’s carnival out of jealousy
Explanation: His tantrum terrorizes others and leaves him no better off.

Event: Patrick threatens to kill SpongeBob over the missing Valentine gift
Explanation: He endangers his friend with no benefit and risks their friendship.

Event: Patrick chases SpongeBob with a jellyfishing net in “Nature Pants”
Explanation: He menaces SpongeBob to force him back, damaging trust without benefit.

Event: Patrick threatens to imprison all of Bikini Bottom when insulted
Explanation: He escalates hostility and fear with no actual gain when power collapses.

Event: Patrick is willing to give Gary a flamethrower “bath”
Explanation: He abuses Gary needlessly and risks trouble with SpongeBob.

Event: Patrick babysits Gary negligently and abusively in “Pet Sitter Pat”
Explanation: His neglect and abuse harm Gary and endanger his relationship with SpongeBob.

Event: Patrick wears shoes on his hands instead of his feet
Explanation: He misuses his shoes, mildly inconveniencing only himself.

Event: Patrick plans with SpongeBob for SpongeBob to pretend to be stupid
Explanation: Patrick manipulates SpongeBob’s image to boost his own standing with parents.

Event: Patrick begins to believe SpongeBob is really stupid and he is a genius
Explanation: His delusion harms his view of SpongeBob and yields no real benefit.

Event: Patrick behaves like a gorilla and is treated as a wild animal
Explanation: His tantrums frighten others and bring him no described advantage.

Event: Patrick blows a raspberry at a ferocious whelk and enrages it
Explanation: He provokes danger for everyone, including himself, without gain.

Event: Patrick dresses as Squidward and gets attacked by scallops
Explanation: His misguided training attempt hurts only him while aiming to help Squidward.

Event: Patrick proposes replacing Krabs’ dollar, then spends his own on candy
Explanation: He preserves Krabs’ original dollar but selfishly uses the replacement on himself.

Event: Patrick dresses as Patricia to hide from a perceived threat
Explanation: Disguise plausibly protects him and harms no one.

Event: Patrick starts taking things as king after SpongeBob says he can have anything
Explanation: He exploits misinterpreted authority to seize others’ property.

Event: Citizens realize Patrick has no power and stop giving him things
Explanation: They regain autonomy and halt their own losses with minimal extra harm to him.

Event: Patrick eats food and then forgets he did so, misattributing theft
Explanation: His forgetfulness harms their trust while giving him no real upside.

Event: Patrick is treated to “starfish treatment” after impersonating a doctor
Explanation: His fraud leads to painful consequences mostly for himself, mildly helping others by exposing him.

Event: Patrick does nothing to help SpongeBob stuck in the wringer and blames him
Explanation: He avoids effort and shifts blame while SpongeBob suffers.

Event: Patrick encourages SpongeBob to get his job in the pilot
Explanation: His advice helps SpongeBob achieve employment and gives Patrick a happier friend.

Event: Patrick frequently throws SpongeBob under the bus
Explanation: He protects his own image at SpongeBob’s expense while keeping the friendship.

Event: Patrick, SpongeBob, and Mr. Krabs try to steal panties from a woman
Explanation: He seeks perverse thrill at her expense, risking serious consequences.

Event: Patrick supports SpongeBob on dangerous quests (movies/games)
Explanation: Tagging along to rescue missions benefits both their goals and bond.

Event: Patrick mouths off to Neptune in defense of SpongeBob, worsening things
Explanation: He bravely but foolishly aggravates a powerful foe, hurting both.

Event: Patrick tries to sacrifice SpongeBob to Lord Poltergeist in “Ghoul Fools”
Explanation: He tries to save himself by offering up his friend to danger.

Event: Patrick crossdresses as Patricia, attracting all the males
Explanation: Disguise protects him and gains attention while misleading others.

Event: Patrick often manipulates SpongeBob into schemes (e.g., Squidtastic Voyage)
Explanation: He nudges SpongeBob into risky plans to satisfy his own curiosity or fun.

Event: Patrick eats from dumpsters and garbage cans
Explanation: He risks his own health for low-grade food with no broader effect.

Event: Patrick continues destructive fits when hearing a bell
Explanation: He becomes uncontrollably dangerous to everyone, including himself.

Event: Patrick reacts with major jealousy when not given a Valentine gift
Explanation: His insecurity spirals into destructive behavior, harming others and himself.

Event: Patrick becomes a ruthless despot as king in “Rule of Dumb”
Explanation: He uses royal status to take and control, hurting subjects while living lavishly.

Event: Patrick destroys the Krusty Krab while fighting Dirty Bubble
Explanation: He causes massive collateral damage for an ultimately accidental victory.

Event: Patrick is willing to sacrifice SpongeBob in “Ghoul Fools”
Explanation: He prioritizes his survival over his friend’s safety.

Event: Patrick crossdresses to avoid perceived dangerous salesman
Explanation: He avoids a perceived threat with a low-cost ruse that harms no one.

Event: Patrick drums well despite stupidity, entertaining others
Explanation: His drumming skill brings him status and enjoyment to others.

Event: Patrick eats a Deluxe Krabby Patty in one bite to show off
Explanation: He impresses others at potential health cost with no real social gain shown.

Event: Patrick steals a donut from SpongeBob, then feels remorse
Explanation: He gains a minor treat then is wracked with guilt, losing more emotionally than he gained.

Event: Patrick zaps SpongeBob with karate, going on a destructive rampage
Explanation: He abuses his new skill to endanger property and even a baby.

Event: Patrick impersonates a doctor in “Suds”
Explanation: His fake medical help worsens SpongeBob’s illness and risks blame.

Event: Patrick copies SpongeBob entirely to win an award in “Big Pink Loser”
Explanation: He aims for recognition by stealing SpongeBob’s identity and work ethic.

Event: Patrick keeps the Krabby Kiddie Meal toy all to himself in “Yours, Mine and Mine”
Explanation: He hogs the shared toy, benefiting at SpongeBob’s expense.

Event: Patrick eats the Patty Pal toy so no one can have it
Explanation: He destroys the toy, depriving everyone including himself.

Event: Patrick neglects Junior in “Rock-a-Bye Bivalve” and refuses to help
Explanation: His laziness burdens SpongeBob and neglects the child scallop.

Event: Patrick feels horrible after seeing the diaper pile and promises to help
Explanation: His remorse leads to genuine support that benefits all three.

Event: Patrick glues SpongeBob into the wringer
Explanation: His carelessness critically harms SpongeBob’s mobility and health.

Event: Patrick gives SpongeBob a rousing “perseverance” speech about the wringer
Explanation: The well-meant speech pushes SpongeBob to endure a clearly harmful situation.

Event: Patrick laughs at SpongeBob’s diary being read and pelts Squidward
Explanation: He joins in cruelty toward SpongeBob and unjustly blames Squidward.

Event: Patrick refuses to accept his own “ugliness” after mocking SpongeBob’s
Explanation: His hypocrisy hurts SpongeBob emotionally and offers him no real gain.

Event: Patrick buys a boat and becomes narcissistic with new power
Explanation: He gains status and enjoyment while treating others worse.

Event: Patrick becomes an insufferable genius after brain coral collision
Explanation: His new intellect isolates him and alienates everyone.

Event: Patrick decides to revert to being dumb by replacing head again
Explanation: He sacrifices intellect to restore his relationships and happiness.

Event: Patrick helps SpongeBob in “Mimic Madness” by defending him
Explanation: He opposes Squidward’s plan to harm SpongeBob, strengthening their bond.

Event: Patrick becomes enraged at Bubble Bass for cheating SpongeBob
Explanation: His anger channels into defending SpongeBob’s fair treatment.

Event: Patrick backs plans that involve forcing Squidward into activities
Explanation: He gains fun while disregarding Squidward’s clear lack of consent.

Event: Patrick mooning to keep Moon Jellyfish from disappearing
Explanation: His action preserves the jellyfish and leads others to help, with minimal cost.

Event: Patrick pretends to be a ghost with SpongeBob using invisible spray
Explanation: He enjoys pranking and scaring the town at their expense.

Event: Patrick often blames others for his mistakes (e.g., “Never My Fault”)
Explanation: He protects his ego while hurting trust and burdening others with blame.

Event: Patrick fails to listen to SpongeBob’s warning about the trading card
Explanation: His inattention ruins SpongeBob’s prized card and damages goodwill.

Event: Patrick tries to sacrifice SpongeBob to save his own hide (explicitly noted)
Explanation: Again chooses self-preservation over loyalty, risking SpongeBob’s safety.

Event: Patrick flies into an Ax-Crazy rage when he thinks his heart is broken
Explanation: Emotional overreaction causes random violence and relationship damage.

Event: Patrick frequently takes advantage of SpongeBob’s generosity
Explanation: He benefits materially and emotionally from SpongeBob while giving little back.

Event: Patrick goes on destructive tantrum when friendship seems threatened
Explanation: His possessiveness leads to chaos that harms others and himself.
